region_of_origin: China (Xinjiang / Northwest China); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Grilled red meat — skewered mutton / lamb

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers lamb kebab as closest parent; no dedicated grilled mutton skewer entry exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers grilled sheep meat as closest parent category; skewer-specific sub-code not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for grilled mutton skewers identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 skewers, each carrying approximately 25 g of seasoned grilled mutton, totalling about 75 g.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 in fat, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17004
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked broiled lamb (composite trimmed retail cuts, lean and fat). Grilled mutton skewers are nutritionally analogous to broiled lamb with cumin and chilli seasoning; seasoning contributes negligible macronutrient change. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical seasoning (salt, cumin, chilli) used in Chinese-style mutton skewers, estimated at approximately 350 mg per 100 g based on recipe-level analysis consistent with USDA data for seasoned grilled lamb.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure grilled meat contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently indicate a GI of 0 for pure meat products with no carbohydrate content. Per Veritas v2 validation rules for zero-carbohydrate foods, `glycemic_index` is set to `1` (the lowest valid positive value, effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; `gi_category` is 'low' per convention (GI 1 ≤ 55 threshold). `glycemic_load_per_serving` = 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero as mutton/lamb is a pure protein-and-fat food with no starch or sugar; this is naturally zero for this food type.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning (cumin, salt, chilli)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 skewers per meal, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 350 mg per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg; requesting reduced salt seasoning is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled mutton skewers contain no digestible carbohydrate and have a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero), making them a suitable high-protein, low-GI option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: With approximately 6.9 g saturated fat and 97 mg cholesterol per 100 g, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to 1–2 skewers per occasion and prefer leaner cuts; the presence of monounsaturated fat (6.8 g per 100 g) is a partially mitigating factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled mutton skewers (烤羊肉串, kǎo yángròu chuàn) are a popular Chinese street food originating from Xinjiang in northwest China, widely enjoyed across China and in Chinese restaurants in Singapore. Small cubes or strips of mutton (sheep meat) are threaded onto bamboo or metal skewers, seasoned generously with ground cumin, dried chilli flakes, salt, and sometimes garlic or sesame, then grilled over charcoal or an open flame until lightly charred on the outside and juicy inside. The result is a smoky, aromatic, mildly spicy skewer with a tender yet slightly chewy texture and a distinctive cumin-forward flavour profile. In Singapore, they are commonly served as a snack or starter, typically in sets of three skewers. Nutritionally, grilled mutton skewers are a high-protein, moderate-fat food with zero carbohydrate content, making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly diets. They provide significant amounts of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and selenium. The moderate saturated fat and sodium content from seasoning warrant portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and when prepared with halal-certified mutton, is suitable for Muslim consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton skewers?
Is 烤羊肉串 high in protein?
How much sodium in mutton skewers?
Mutton kebab vs lamb kebab nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy broiled lamb data, which is the closest approved-source match for grilled mutton. No exact Singapore-specific or HPB Foodo entry for grilled mutton skewers was identified. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical seasoning. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as mutton is a pure protein-and-fat food containing no starch, sugar, or digestible carbohydrate. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as there is no plant-derived fibre in plain grilled mutton. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lamb. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lamb. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates, per Veritas v2 validation rules for zero-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is 'low' per convention (GI 1 ≤ 55 threshold). glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate per serving is 0 g. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 6.8 sourced from USDA SR Legacy). polyunsaturated_fat_g value 1.2 sourced from USDA SR Legacy. trans_fat_g value 0.5 reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid in ruminant fat per USDA SR Legacy. Sodium of 350 mg per 100 g is an upward adaptation from unseasoned broiled lamb (~75 mg per 100 g) to account for salt, cumin, and chilli seasoning typical of Chinese-style mutton skewers; this is an estimation and should be verified against laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. FoodOn match FOODON_03302051 is closest_parent (lamb kebab food product); no exact grilled mutton skewer entry exists. FoodEx2 A0FKP is closest_parent (grilled sheep meat). SNOMED CT is null as no specific concept was identified. Input food_id GD003841 and restaurant source (Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant, Circular Road, Singapore) preserved from seed record.
